Article.ArticleTitle: Effects of excess curdlan on the localized phase separation and gel strength deterioration of myofibrillar protein under different ionic strengths evidenced by Df and microrheology.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study investigates the intrinsic relationships between the phase separation behavior of myofibrillar protein (MP)/curdlan (CUR) mixtures and their gel properties under varying ionic strengths and CUR concentrations. Microstructural analysis demonstrated that at CUR concentrations below 4%, MP acted as the primary gel matrix, forming a dense composite network. At concentrations exceeding 4%, self-aggregation occurred, resulting in a loose network structure. Microrheological results combined with reduced gel mechanical strength and decreased Df values, confirmed the occurrence of localized phase separation. Across different polysaccharide concentrations, gels formed under 0.1 mol/L NaCl exhibited superior stress response, compared with those under higher salt conditions. This observation suggests that salt ions inhibit the hydration and structural disordering of CUR, thereby affecting the overall hardness and water-holding capacity of the gel. 4% CUR represents the critical concentration in the mixed system, with improved gel properties observed under low-salt conditions.
